heroOverview: Pallor is an abnormal reduction in skin and mucosal colour, reflecting either decreased haemoglobin concentration in the blood, reduced blood flow to the skin, or in rare cases, reduced melanin pigmentation. It is one of the most common clinical signs encountered across all age groups and medical settings — anaemia alone, the leading systemic cause, affects approximately 1.62 billion people worldwide, representing 24.8% of the global population. While pallor visible in the conjunctivae or palmar creases strongly suggests clinically significant anaemia (haemoglobin typically below 9 g/dL), pallor is also a key sign in haemorrhagic shock, cardiogenic shock, vasovagal syncope, and severe hypothyroidism. The significance of pallor ranges from the trivial — fair complexion, cold ambient temperature — to the life-threatening, such as acute haemorrhage or leukaemic crisis. Systematic assessment of the likely underlying cause determines urgency and guides investigation.

keyStats.prevalence: Anaemia, the most common cause, affects approximately 24.8% of the world population — about 1.62 billion people (WHO Global Anaemia Estimates)
keyStats.demographics: Anaemia-related pallor most prevalent in preschool children (47.4%), pregnant women (41.8%), and non-pregnant women of reproductive age (30.2%); pallor from other causes affects all ages
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; iron-deficiency anaemia peaks in young women and elderly; leukaemia-related pallor in children and older adults; shock-related pallor in all ages
keyStats.globalCases: Iron-deficiency anaemia alone accounts for approximately 50% of all anaemia cases globally, affecting over 800 million people

definition: Pallor (ICD-10: R23.1) is a clinical sign characterised by decreased pink or red colouration of the skin, mucous membranes, and conjunctivae below what is normal for a given individual. The normal colour of skin and mucosae reflects two components: the concentration and oxygenation state of haemoglobin in subepidermal capillaries, and the quantity of melanin pigmentation in the epidermis. Pallor as a pathological sign results from reduction in haemoglobin (anaemia from any cause), reduction in cutaneous blood flow (vasoconstriction in shock, hypothermia, sympathetic activation, or peripheral vascular disease), or reduced haemoglobin oxygenation (although this produces more of a grey or cyanotic hue). The most reliable clinical sites for detecting pallor independent of baseline skin pigmentation — which is critical in darker-skinned individuals — are the palpebral conjunctivae (inner lower eyelid), the palmar creases, the oral mucosa, and the nail beds. Pallor of the conjunctivae has a positive likelihood ratio of approximately 4.5 for detecting haemoglobin below 8 g/dL, making it the most clinically useful site. Pallor is a presenting sign spanning multiple specialties: general medicine, haematology, emergency medicine, and surgery. It falls under general medicine in this classification (ICD R23.1, a symptom code) and its management is driven by the underlying diagnosis.

typesClassification.description: Caused by reduced haemoglobin concentration — from iron deficiency, B12 or folate deficiency, chronic disease, haemolysis, bone marrow failure, or leukaemia; pallor is diffuse, affecting all mucosae and skin beds.

Caused by reduced cutaneous blood flow from cold exposure, shock (hypovolaemic, cardiogenic, or distributive), vasovagal syncope, or anxiety; skin is pale and often cold and clammy rather than simply pale.
Confined to a limb or segment from arterial occlusion, Raynaud phenomenon, or severe peripheral arterial disease; the pallor is asymmetric and associated with loss of pulse.
Normal variant in fair-skinned, light-pigmented individuals; haemoglobin is normal, conjunctivae and mucosae have normal pink colouration; pallor is apparent only in the skin surface.

primarySymptoms: Visible paleness of the skin, particularly on the face, lips, and fingernails, which prompts the initial clinical concern.
Pale or white conjunctivae — the inner surface of the lower eyelid, which is the most reliable sign of anaemic pallor regardless of skin pigmentation.
Fatigue and reduced exercise capacity, proportional to the severity of anaemia; the muscles receive less oxygen with each unit of blood pumped.
Shortness of breath on exertion — the heart compensates for reduced oxygen-carrying capacity by increasing stroke volume and heart rate, leading to dyspnoea with effort.
Palpitations or an awareness of a fast or strong heartbeat, as tachycardia is the earliest cardiovascular compensation for falling haemoglobin.
Dizziness or lightheadedness, particularly on standing (orthostatic), from inadequate cerebral oxygenation relative to demand.
Headache — a common symptom of moderate to severe anaemia, likely from cerebrovascular compensatory vasodilation.
Cold skin and extremities in pallor due to peripheral vasoconstriction and shock, as blood is shunted to vital organs.
Pallor of the palmar creases — when creases are white rather than pink, haemoglobin is typically below 7 g/dL in adults.
Glossitis, angular cheilitis, or koilonychia (spoon nails) in iron-deficiency anaemia, which also produces pallor alongside these specific examination findings.
earlyWarningSigns: Fatigue that is new, unexplained, and persists beyond what exercise or poor sleep would explain.
Noticing lips or fingernails look paler or more washed-out than usual.
Slightly faster heartbeat at rest or more breathlessness than expected on stairs.
Pallor first noticed by a family member or healthcare professional during a routine consultation.
emergencySigns: Sudden-onset severe pallor with hypotension, rapid weak pulse, and confusion — indicates haemorrhagic or cardiogenic shock requiring emergency resuscitation.
Pallor with high fever and bruising or bleeding from multiple sites — may indicate acute leukaemia or aplastic anaemia, requiring urgent haematological assessment.
Pallor with severe chest pain and sweating in adults — consistent with acute myocardial infarction causing cardiogenic shock.
Pallor following major trauma — acute haemorrhage and hypovolaemic shock require immediate surgical intervention.
Pallor in a child with severe respiratory distress, altered consciousness, or hepatosplenomegaly — requires emergency evaluation.

causes.description: The most common cause of pallor worldwide; dietary insufficiency, chronic blood loss (heavy menstruation, gastrointestinal bleeding), or malabsorption depletes iron stores, reducing haemoglobin synthesis; the anaemia develops gradually, and pallor may be the first visible sign noticed by the patient or clinician.

causes.cause: Vitamin B12 or folate deficiency anaemia
causes.description: Megaloblastic anaemia from dietary deficiency, malabsorption (pernicious anaemia, coeliac disease), or certain medications (methotrexate, metformin) impairs DNA synthesis in red cell precursors, producing large but fragile cells; pallor is accompanied by macrocytosis on the blood film and neurological symptoms in B12 deficiency.

Premature destruction of red blood cells — from inherited conditions (sickle cell disease, thalassaemia, hereditary spherocytosis) or acquired causes (autoimmune haemolysis, infections, drug reactions) — reduces the circulating red cell lifespan; pallor is accompanied by jaundice from bilirubin release and sometimes splenomegaly.

Acute reduction in cardiac output or circulating volume triggers intense sympathetic vasoconstriction that diverts blood to vital organs; the skin becomes pale, cold, and clammy; this cause of pallor is the most life-threatening and presents within minutes of the precipitating event.

causes.cause: Leukaemia and bone marrow failure (aplastic anaemia)
causes.description: Infiltration of the bone marrow by malignant cells (leukaemia, lymphoma) or autoimmune destruction of stem cells (aplastic anaemia) suppresses red cell production; pallor develops alongside thrombocytopenic bruising or bleeding and neutropenic infections.

The most common anaemia in hospital inpatients; inflammatory cytokines from chronic infection, autoimmune disease, or malignancy inhibit iron utilisation and erythropoiesis; pallor is mild to moderate and accompanied by the systemic features of the underlying condition.

Reduced thyroid hormone slows erythropoiesis and may contribute to a mild normocytic or macrocytic anaemia; the pallor in hypothyroidism often has a waxy, yellowish quality from accumulation of carotene, and is accompanied by other features of underactive thyroid — fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, and bradycardia.

riskFactors.description: Menstrual blood loss of more than 80 mL per cycle significantly depletes iron stores; women of reproductive age are the group most affected by iron-deficiency anaemia globally, with rates of 30-50% in some regions.

Expanded plasma volume dilutes haemoglobin concentration, and the developing foetus has high iron demands; pregnant women are at high risk of anaemia, and anaemia in pregnancy is associated with preterm birth and low birth weight.

Vegetarian and vegan diets, food insecurity, and low intake of haem iron sources (red meat, organ meats) all reduce daily iron absorption; non-haem iron from plant sources is absorbed at only 1-10% compared to 15-35% for haem iron.

Peptic ulcer disease, colorectal cancer, angiodysplasia, and NSAID use are leading causes of chronic occult gastrointestinal bleeding that depletes iron faster than it can be replaced; pallor from this cause should always prompt colonoscopy and gastroscopy in adults over 50.

Failing kidneys produce insufficient erythropoietin, the hormone that stimulates red cell production; anaemia from CKD is common from stage 3 onwards and contributes to cardiovascular risk; it responds to erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs).

riskFactors.factor: Family history of haemolytic or inherited anaemia
riskFactors.description: Sickle cell disease, thalassaemia, and hereditary spherocytosis are inherited haemolytic anaemias that cause chronic pallor from haemolysis; newborn screening and genetic counselling are standard in affected populations.

Anaemia affects approximately 10-24% of adults over 65; causes include chronic disease, CKD, nutritional deficiency, and haematological malignancy; even mild anaemia in older adults reduces exercise capacity, increases fall risk, and raises cardiovascular mortality.

Leukaemia, lymphoma, and multiple myeloma directly suppress normal haemopoiesis; pallor in a patient with unexplained fever, bruising, or persistent fatigue requires urgent blood count and haematological assessment.

affectedDemographics: Preschool children, who have high iron demands and frequent dietary deficiency
Adolescent girls and young women with heavy menstrual loss
Pregnant women in their second and third trimesters
Elderly adults with chronic disease, nutritional deficiency, or malignancy
People from regions with high prevalence of malaria, parasitic infections, or inherited haemoglobinopathies
Patients with chronic kidney disease stage 3 or higher
Vegetarians and vegans without dietary supplementation
Patients receiving chemotherapy or immunosuppressive therapy
diagnosisOverview: Clinical assessment of pallor begins with inspection of the conjunctivae, palmar creases, nail beds, and oral mucosae — sites where haemoglobin-derived colour can be assessed independent of skin tone. Conjunctival pallor has the highest diagnostic accuracy: pale conjunctivae strongly suggest haemoglobin below 8-9 g/dL. A full blood count (FBC) is the essential first investigation: it confirms the haemoglobin level, red cell indices (MCV and MCH for microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic patterns), white cell count (leukaemia screening), and platelet count. The red cell indices guide the next step — microcytic anaemia prompts iron studies (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC); macrocytic anaemia triggers B12 and folate levels; normocytic anaemia prompts reticulocyte count, renal function, inflammatory markers, and thyroid function. A peripheral blood film examined by a haematologist adds detail: hypochromic microcytes indicate iron deficiency; macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils indicate megaloblastic anaemia; schistocytes indicate haemolysis; blast cells indicate acute leukaemia. Reticulocyte count distinguishes hypoproliferative (low reticulocyte) from haemolytic (high reticulocyte) anaemia. In pallor with systemic features suggesting malignancy — weight loss, lymphadenopathy, unexplained fever — a bone marrow biopsy may be needed. Pallor in the context of acute haemodynamic compromise requires no delay for laboratory results: blood pressure, pulse, and Glasgow Coma Scale determine urgency, and resuscitation is initiated on clinical grounds.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The cornerstone investigation: measures haemoglobin, haematocrit, red cell indices, white cell and platelet counts to confirm anaemia and give immediate direction to the cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw; results available within 1-2 hours in most settings; the most important single test in pallor evaluation.

diagnosticTests.test: Iron studies (serum ferritin, serum iron, TIBC)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes iron-deficiency anaemia (low ferritin, low iron, high TIBC) from anaemia of chronic disease (low iron but normal or high ferritin, low TIBC); essential for microcytic hypochromic anaemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous blood draw ideally in the fasting state, as serum iron rises after a meal; results available within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies megaloblastic anaemia in macrocytic pallor; low B12 can also cause neurological damage independent of anaemia, making early detection important.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw; results available within 1-2 days; intrinsic factor and parietal cell antibodies are added when pernicious anaemia is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visual examination by a haematologist of red cell morphology, white cells, and platelets; identifies specific patterns (blast cells, spherocytes, sickle cells, schistocytes) that narrow the differential diagnosis of pallor.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The same blood sample as the FBC is processed into a glass slide; a haematologist reviews the film within 24-48 hours and adds morphological detail.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the proportion of young red cells; distinguishes haemolytic or haemorrhagic anaemia (high reticulocytes — bone marrow responding actively) from bone marrow failure or nutritional deficiency (low reticulocytes — impaired production).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Added to a standard blood count sample; results same day; the reticulocyte index corrects for haematocrit to give a more meaningful value.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hypothyroidism, which causes pallor, normocytic or macrocytic anaemia, and a range of systemic symptoms; TSH is the most sensitive screening test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous blood draw; TSH results available within 4-8 hours in most laboratories; a suppressed TSH suggests hyperthyroidism, a raised TSH hypothyroidism.

diagnosticTests.test: Renal function (creatinine, eGFR) and erythropoietin level
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies chronic kidney disease as a cause of normocytic anaemia from erythropoietin deficiency; eGFR below 30 ml/min/1.73m2 is strongly associated with clinically significant anaemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous blood draw; creatinine and eGFR results available within hours; erythropoietin assay is specialised and takes 2-5 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of pallor is entirely directed at the underlying cause — pallor itself is a sign, not a disease. For the most common cause, iron-deficiency anaemia, oral iron supplementation is first-line: ferrous sulfate 200 mg (65 mg elemental iron) two to three times daily, taken on an empty stomach to maximise absorption, with ascorbic acid to enhance uptake. A rise of 2 g/dL in haemoglobin within 4 weeks of starting iron confirms a satisfactory response; treatment continues for 3-6 months after haemoglobin normalisation to replenish iron stores. Intravenous iron (ferric carboxymaltose, ferric derisomaltose) is used when oral iron is not tolerated, absorption is impaired, or rapid correction is needed. B12 deficiency is treated with intramuscular hydroxocobalamin or, in dietary deficiency, oral cyanocobalamin; folate deficiency with oral folic acid 5 mg daily. Anaemia of chronic kidney disease is managed with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) such as darbepoetin alfa, titrated to a haemoglobin target of 10-12 g/dL, combined with IV iron. Haematological malignancy causing pallor requires specialist haematology management: leukaemia with chemotherapy ± stem cell transplant; aplastic anaemia with immunosuppression or transplant. In acute haemorrhagic shock, resuscitation with IV crystalloid and red blood cell transfusion targeting haemoglobin above 7-8 g/dL (or 10 g/dL in cardiovascular disease) is standard. Pallor from vasoconstrictive causes (vasovagal syncope, anxiety) resolves with addressing the precipitant and does not require specific medical treatment. Red blood cell transfusion provides immediate but temporary correction — it is a bridge while the underlying cause is treated, not a standalone long-term strategy.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate, ferrous gluconate, ferrous fumarate)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for iron-deficiency anaemia; oral ferrous sulfate 200 mg twice or three times daily provides 130-195 mg elemental iron, sufficient to restore haemoglobin at 2 g/dL per month with adequate absorption; taken on an empty stomach 1 hour before meals for maximum uptake.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haemoglobin rises by approximately 1-2 g/dL per week and reaches normal within 6-8 weeks in uncomplicated iron deficiency; full iron store replenishment requires 3-6 months of continued therapy after haemoglobin normalises.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous iron (ferric carboxymaltose, ferric derisomaltose)
medicalTreatments.description: Recommended when oral iron fails due to side effects, malabsorption (coeliac disease, post-gastric bypass), inflammatory bowel disease, or when rapid correction is needed pre-operatively; a single dose of 500-1000 mg can be administered in 15-30 minutes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves haemoglobin normalisation in 4-8 weeks in approximately 80-90% of patients with iron-deficiency anaemia; superior to oral iron in inflammatory bowel disease and chronic kidney disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Intramuscular or oral vitamin B12 (hydroxocobalamin)
medicalTreatments.description: For pernicious anaemia or other B12 absorption defects, intramuscular hydroxocobalamin is given as a loading course (6 injections over 2 weeks) then maintenance every 3 months for life; oral B12 at high dose (1000 mcg daily) is effective in dietary deficiency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haemoglobin begins rising within 1-2 weeks of starting B12 replacement; MCV normalises within 8-12 weeks; neurological symptoms respond more slowly and may not fully reverse if longstanding.

medicalTreatments.name: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (darbepoetin alfa, epoetin alfa) for CKD anaemia
medicalTreatments.description: Administered subcutaneously every 1-4 weeks in chronic kidney disease anaemia from erythropoietin deficiency; dosed to target haemoglobin of 10-12 g/dL; must be combined with adequate iron stores for full response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Increases haemoglobin by approximately 1.5-2.5 g/dL above baseline in CKD patients with adequate iron; reduces transfusion dependency in approximately 70-80% of treated patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Red blood cell transfusion
medicalTreatments.description: Provides immediate temporary correction of severe symptomatic anaemia (haemoglobin below 7 g/dL, or below 8-10 g/dL in cardiovascular disease) or in haemorrhagic shock; does not correct the underlying cause and carries risks of transfusion reactions and iron overload with repeated use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Each unit of packed red blood cells raises haemoglobin by approximately 1 g/dL; provides immediate improvement in oxygen-carrying capacity; effect lasts until endogenous red cell production is restored or the underlying cause is corrected.

medicalTreatments.name: Folic acid supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Oral folic acid 5 mg daily corrects folate-deficiency macrocytic anaemia; lower doses (400 mcg) are used preventively in pregnancy to reduce neural tube defects; B12 must be excluded before starting folic acid alone, as it can mask B12 neuropathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haemoglobin rises measurably within 2-4 weeks; reticulocyte response peaks at 7-10 days in pure folate deficiency, confirming the diagnosis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary iron optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Increasing dietary haem iron (red meat, organ meats, shellfish) alongside vitamin C-containing foods to enhance non-haem iron absorption from plant sources; effective for mild deficiency and as an adjunct to supplementation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Management of precipitating conditions (e.g. menorrhagia treatment)
alternativeTreatments.description: For recurrent iron-deficiency anaemia from heavy periods, hormonal treatment or transcervical endometrial resection reduces blood loss substantially and breaks the cycle of ongoing iron depletion; addressing the cause is more sustainable than indefinite supplementation alone.

whySeeSpecialist: Pallor is a common presenting sign warranting urgent specialist input in specific circumstances: haemoglobin below 7 g/dL in a symptomatic patient; unexplained pallor with abnormal white cell or platelet count (possible haematological malignancy); pallor with haemodynamic compromise (shock); or failure of an expected response to initial iron or B12 therapy within 4-6 weeks. A haematologist should assess all cases of suspected bone marrow disease. A gastroenterologist should investigate iron-deficiency anaemia in men and post-menopausal women where a gastrointestinal source must be excluded.
doctorSelectionGuide: The appropriate specialist depends on the identified cause: a haematologist for unexplained, severe, or malignancy-related anaemia; a gastroenterologist for iron-deficiency with suspected occult gastrointestinal bleeding; a nephrologist for CKD-related anaemia; a gynaecologist for menorrhagia-driven iron loss; a general physician or internist for multifactorial or anaemia of chronic disease. In paediatric pallor, a paediatric haematologist should be involved early when structural haemoglobin disorders are suspected.
preventionStrategies: Ensure adequate dietary iron, B12, and folate intake throughout life; supplementation is especially important in pregnancy, infancy, and adolescence.
Screen for and treat menorrhagia to prevent cumulative iron loss in women of reproductive age.
All pregnant women should receive haemoglobin screening at booking and at 28 weeks; iron supplementation is given when haemoglobin falls below 10.5 g/dL.
Screen adults over 50 with iron-deficiency anaemia for gastrointestinal cancer — this is a red-flag investigation, not an optional one.
In patients with chronic kidney disease, monitor haemoglobin annually from stage 3 and initiate ESA therapy when anaemia becomes symptomatic or haemoglobin falls below 10 g/dL.
Neonatal and childhood screening for sickle cell and thalassaemia in endemic populations allows early intervention before severe pallor and anaemia develop.
lifestyleModifications: Consume iron-rich foods daily: lean red meat, chicken liver, kidney beans, lentils, fortified cereals, and dark leafy greens.
Pair plant-based iron sources with vitamin C (orange juice, tomatoes) to enhance non-haem iron absorption by up to three times.
Avoid drinking tea or coffee with iron-containing meals; tannins bind iron and reduce absorption by 50-60%.
Space calcium supplements and iron supplements at least 2 hours apart, as calcium blocks iron absorption.
If taking oral iron, be prepared for constipation — increase fibre and fluid intake; ferrous gluconate is better tolerated than ferrous sulfate in some patients.
Follow up blood counts until haemoglobin is confirmed normal and iron stores replenished — symptom resolution precedes full store replenishment.

recommended: Haem iron sources: red meat (3-4 servings weekly), organ meats, shellfish (especially oysters and clams)
Non-haem iron sources: lentils, kidney beans, tofu, fortified breakfast cereals, dark leafy greens
Vitamin C at every meal to enhance non-haem iron absorption: citrus fruit, capsicum, kiwi fruit, tomatoes
B12-containing foods: meat, fish, dairy, eggs; B12-fortified plant milks and cereals for vegans
Folate-rich foods: dark leafy greens, legumes, avocado, fortified cereals
avoid: Tea and coffee with iron-rich meals — tannins chelate iron and reduce absorption
High-calcium foods or supplements immediately around iron-rich meals
Phytate-rich foods (high-fibre grains, raw legumes) consumed in large amounts alongside the main iron source of the day
Excessive alcohol, which impairs B12 absorption and suppresses erythropoiesis

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise tolerance in anaemic pallor is directly proportional to haemoglobin level. At haemoglobin above 10 g/dL, moderate exercise is generally well tolerated; below 8-9 g/dL, patients are typically limited to light activity. Breathlessness on mild exertion, palpitations at rest, or chest pain with activity are symptoms to act on — seek medical review rather than pushing through. As haemoglobin rises with treatment, exercise capacity improves measurably, and structured graded exercise resumption is appropriate. In shock-related pallor, rest and resuscitation take priority; exercise is entirely contraindicated until haemodynamics are restored.
dailyManagement: Take oral iron supplements consistently — twice or three times daily as prescribed; missing doses significantly slows recovery.
Track symptoms: energy levels, breathlessness, and palpitations improve in parallel with rising haemoglobin; record changes to report at follow-up.
Attend all follow-up blood count appointments; haemoglobin monitoring at 4 weeks confirms treatment response.
Report any new symptoms during iron therapy: black tarry stools may indicate gastrointestinal bleeding masked by iron, not just from the iron supplement itself.
Continue B12 or folate supplementation for the full prescribed duration even after feeling well; stopping early leads to relapse.
prognosis: The prognosis of pallor depends entirely on the underlying cause. Iron-deficiency anaemia corrects fully with oral or intravenous iron in the great majority of patients; haemoglobin normalises within 6-8 weeks and quality of life improves measurably within 2-4 weeks of starting treatment. B12 deficiency anaemia is readily reversible with replacement; neurological deficits from B12 deficiency may only partially reverse if they have been present for months. Anaemia of chronic kidney disease improves substantially with ESA and iron therapy, and is an independent determinant of cardiovascular mortality in this population — its treatment reduces hospitalisation and improves quality of life. Pallor from haematological malignancy carries the prognosis of the malignancy: acute leukaemia mortality at 5 years ranges from 25-70% depending on type and age; aplastic anaemia with modern immunosuppression or stem cell transplant achieves long-term survival in over 80% of treated patients. Pallor from acute haemorrhagic shock carries high mortality without immediate resuscitation but is fully reversible when blood loss is controlled rapidly.
recoveryTimeline: In iron-deficiency anaemia, symptoms of fatigue and breathlessness begin to improve within 1-2 weeks of starting iron supplementation as reticulocyte production increases; haemoglobin rises approximately 1-2 g/dL per month and reaches the normal range in 6-8 weeks. Iron stores require a further 3-6 months of therapy to replenish. B12 deficiency anaemia shows a reticulocyte surge within 7-10 days of replacement, with haemoglobin normalising within 6-8 weeks. Folate deficiency responds similarly. CKD anaemia improves over 4-12 weeks of ESA therapy, with the time to response varying by dose and adequacy of iron stores.
complications: Heart failure and high-output cardiac failure — severe chronic anaemia forces the heart to increase output compensatorily; prolonged severe pallor from untreated anaemia can precipitate heart failure, particularly in those with pre-existing coronary disease.

Cognitive impairment and developmental delay — iron-deficiency anaemia in infants and young children impairs cognitive development even after haemoglobin is corrected; prevention is more effective than treatment of already established deficits.

Pregnancy complications — anaemia in pregnancy is associated with low birth weight, preterm delivery, and maternal fatigue; severe peripartum haemorrhage is more dangerous in an already anaemic mother.
Increased surgical risk — pallor from significant anaemia before elective surgery raises perioperative mortality; preoperative optimisation with IV iron or ESA is standard practice.
Progression of underlying malignancy — pallor from leukaemia or aplastic anaemia is a sign of advanced bone marrow compromise; treatment delay worsens outcomes substantially.

supportResources.name: NHS — Anaemia
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK patient guide to the most common causes of pallor and anaemia, including iron-deficiency and vitamin B12 deficiency, with treatment and dietary advice.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Anemia
supportResources.description: U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases patient overview of anaemia types, causes, diagnosis, and treatment.

supportResources.name: CDC — Iron Deficiency Anaemia
supportResources.description: CDC information on iron-deficiency anaemia prevalence, risk groups, and prevention strategies.

sources.title: WHO — The Global Prevalence of Anaemia in 2011. World Health Organization 2015
Gaskell H et al. Capsule endoscopy versus other investigations in the evaluation of obscure gastrointestinal bleeding. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2018
Stabler SP. Vitamin B12 deficiency. NEJM 2013
Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) Anaemia Guidelines 2012
Chaparro CM, Suchdev PS. Anemia epidemiology, pathophysiology, and etiology in low- and middle-income countries. Ann N Y Acad Sci 2019

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia (general)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaemia is the most common underlying cause of pallor but is itself a finding (low haemoglobin), not synonymous with pallor; pallor is the visible sign, anaemia is the laboratory-confirmed diagnosis — pallor can also result from vasoconstriction without anaemia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism produces a waxy, yellowish pallor from carotene accumulation alongside fatigue and cold intolerance; the pallor can mislead toward iron-deficiency anaemia, but thyroid function tests distinguish them, and both conditions can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leukaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leukaemia causes pallor through bone marrow infiltration suppressing red cell production; the distinguishing features are associated bruising, bleeding, lymphadenopathy, and a full blood count showing blast cells, which are absent in nutritional anaemia.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common systemic cause of pallor; identifying anaemia on a blood count is usually the first step in evaluating pallor and triggers the search for the specific anaemia type.

relatedConditions.name: Iron deficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: Iron deficiency without frank anaemia causes pallor, fatigue, and reduced exercise capacity; serum ferritin is a sensitive marker before haemoglobin falls.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Thyroid hormone deficiency suppresses erythropoiesis and causes pallor accompanied by fatigue, cold intolerance, and bradycardia; easily missed as a cause of pallor if thyroid function is not checked.

relatedConditions.name: Leukaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Haematological malignancy suppresses normal red cell production and is a critical cause of pallor to identify; unexplained pallor with abnormal white count or platelets requires urgent haematology review.

faqs.question: What causes pallor?
faqs.answer: Pallor is most commonly caused by anaemia — reduced haemoglobin from iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, chronic disease, or bone marrow failure. It is also caused by reduced blood flow to the skin in shock, cold exposure, or vasovagal reactions. A full blood count is the key initial test to identify the underlying cause.

faqs.question: Is pallor a sign of anaemia?
faqs.answer: Yes — anaemia is the most common systemic cause of pallor. Pale conjunctivae (inner lower eyelid) and pale palmar creases are the most reliable clinical signs; conjunctival pallor predicts haemoglobin below 8-9 g/dL with reasonable accuracy. However, a blood count is required to confirm and quantify anaemia.

faqs.question: How do you check for pallor?
faqs.answer: The best sites to check for pallor are the inner surface of the lower eyelid (palpebral conjunctiva), which should be pink; the palmar creases, which should be pink even in a closed fist; the oral mucosa; and the nail beds. These sites are most reliable across all skin tones.

faqs.question: When is pallor an emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care immediately if pallor appears suddenly with sweating, rapid weak pulse, low blood pressure, or confusion — signs of shock. Also act urgently if pallor accompanies chest pain, heavy bleeding, or in a child with fever, bruising, and swollen lymph nodes, which may indicate leukaemia.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for pallor?
faqs.answer: Treatment targets the underlying cause. Iron-deficiency anaemia is treated with oral iron supplements for 3-6 months. B12 deficiency is treated with injections or high-dose oral B12. Chronic kidney disease anaemia needs erythropoiesis-stimulating agents. Severe acute anaemia may require a red blood cell transfusion.

faqs.question: Can pallor be normal?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fair-skinned or light-complexioned people may appear pale without any medical problem; their conjunctivae and mucosae will be normally pink. Constitutional pallor requires no treatment. The clinical concern is new pallor, especially when the person or family notices a change, or when conjunctival or mucosal pallor is seen.

faqs.question: What blood test shows pallor causes?
faqs.answer: A full blood count (FBC) is the essential test — it measures haemoglobin, red cell size, white cells, and platelets. Iron studies (ferritin, serum iron) are added for microcytic anaemia; B12 and folate for macrocytic; renal function and TSH for normocytic anaemia. A peripheral blood film adds morphological detail.

faqs.question: Can iron deficiency cause pallor without anaemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Iron depletion reduces the haem precursors available for red cell production before haemoglobin falls below the anaemia threshold. Serum ferritin below 30 mcg/L can cause fatigue and pallor with a technically normal haemoglobin; treating the iron deficiency resolves symptoms.

faqs.question: How quickly does pallor improve with iron treatment?
faqs.answer: Symptoms such as fatigue begin to improve within 1-2 weeks of starting iron supplements. Haemoglobin rises by approximately 1-2 g/dL per month and reaches normal within 6-8 weeks in uncomplicated iron deficiency. Iron stores take a further 3-6 months of supplementation to fully replenish.

faqs.question: Can dehydration cause pallor?
faqs.answer: Mild dehydration alone does not typically cause significant pallor, but severe dehydration reduces circulating volume and triggers vasoconstriction, causing pale clammy skin as part of a shock state. Rehydration corrects the pallor in this context. Haemoglobin concentration may appear artificially elevated in dehydration, masking underlying anaemia.

faqs.question: Is pallor a sign of leukaemia?
faqs.answer: Pallor can be an early sign of leukaemia, caused by bone marrow replacement suppressing red cell production. The key distinguishing features are associated bruising from low platelets, recurrent infections from neutropenia, weight loss, and lymph node enlargement — alongside a blood count showing blast cells or abnormal cell counts.

faqs.question: Why does my child look pale?
faqs.answer: In children, pallor is most commonly caused by iron-deficiency anaemia from a diet low in iron-rich foods, or from a period of rapid growth with increased iron demands. Other causes include viral illness, chronic infection, or, rarely, leukaemia. A blood count and iron studies are the appropriate starting point for any child with persistent unexplained pallor.

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause pallor?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anxiety and fear trigger the sympathetic nervous system, causing vasoconstriction of skin blood vessels — the face, lips, and extremities become pale, often with cold clammy skin. This is the same mechanism as vasovagal pallor before fainting. It resolves when the anxiety episode passes and does not indicate anaemia.

faqs.question: Does hypothyroidism cause pallor?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypothyroidism reduces erythropoietin production and slows the bone marrow's red cell output, causing a mild to moderate anaemia with a distinctive waxy, slightly yellowish pallor. The thyroid-induced pallor is accompanied by fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, and weight gain — checking TSH alongside a blood count identifies this cause.

faqs.question: Can vitamin B12 deficiency cause pallor?
faqs.answer: Yes. B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anaemia where large, fragile red blood cells are produced inefficiently, reducing the oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood and causing pallor. B12 deficiency also causes neurological symptoms including tingling, numbness, and memory difficulties — these can be irreversible if treatment is delayed.

faqs.question: What foods help with pallor from anaemia?
faqs.answer: For iron-deficiency anaemia: red meat, organ meats, shellfish, lentils, beans, and fortified cereals are highest in iron. Vitamin C-containing foods eaten at the same meal triple non-haem iron absorption. Avoid tea and coffee at iron-rich meals. For B12 deficiency: meat, fish, eggs, and dairy; vegans need B12-fortified foods or supplements.

faqs.question: Is pallor in pregnancy normal?
faqs.answer: Some degree of pallor is common in pregnancy because expanded plasma volume dilutes haemoglobin (physiological dilutional anaemia). However, haemoglobin below 10.5 g/dL in pregnancy requires investigation and usually iron supplementation. Severe pallor with breathlessness or palpitations in pregnancy should be assessed the same day.

faqs.question: When does pallor need a blood transfusion?
faqs.answer: Red blood cell transfusion is generally indicated when haemoglobin falls below 7 g/dL in otherwise healthy adults, or below 8-10 g/dL in those with cardiovascular disease or active symptoms. Emergency transfusion is used for acute haemorrhagic shock regardless of haemoglobin threshold. Transfusion is a bridge — the underlying cause must still be treated.

faqs.question: Can CKD cause pallor?
faqs.answer: Yes. From CKD stage 3 onwards, the damaged kidneys produce insufficient erythropoietin, resulting in normocytic anaemia and pallor. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) and IV iron are the standard treatment; anaemia from CKD contributes to cardiovascular risk and reduced exercise tolerance independent of kidney function.

faqs.question: What does pale conjunctiva mean?
faqs.answer: Pale or white conjunctivae — the inner surface of the lower eyelid — is the most reliable clinical sign of significant anaemia. Pink conjunctivae are normal. Pallor of the conjunctivae typically indicates haemoglobin below 8-9 g/dL and should always prompt a full blood count. It can be assessed in people of all skin tones without the artefacts of skin pigmentation.

metaDescription: Pallor means abnormal paleness from anaemia, shock, or poor circulation. Learn how to assess it, what causes it, and when to seek urgent medical care.
